metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the return type and shape of `compute_validity` (e.g., `{"valid": bool, "reason": str}` or a named dataclass), including what is returned when the attestation is absent.
findings.finding: The return type/shape of `compute_validity(attestation, ticket_state, kind)` is never specified. The plan calls it a 'dispatcher' that 'centralizes per-kind validity' but does not state what it returns (boolean, verdict dict, validity record, or exception). The close-gate path, `verify_signature`, and the child-closure path all presumably consume this, yet each would need to know the return contract.
findings.location: Validity model section / AC line 5 (`compute_validity` description)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Validity model section: '`compute_validity` returns a dict `{"certified": bool, "valid": bool, "reason": str}` where `certified` reflects the HMAC check result and `valid` reflects all per-kind freshness/state rules; returns `{"certified": False, "valid": False, "reason": "not found"}` when the attestation is absent.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the return schema for `compute_validity(attestation, ticket_state, kind)` — specify at minimum the output keys (`valid: bool`, `reason: str`, and any extra per-kind fields) so that cc1b/verify_signature and the gate-check callers share a single contract.
findings.finding: `compute_validity(attestation, ticket_state, kind)` is referenced as a new central entry-point, but its return type/schema is never defined. Callers (the `verify_signature` path, gate checks) need to know if it returns a boolean, a `{valid, reason}` dict, or something else — an implementer will choose arbitrarily.
findings.location: Validity model section; AC bullet 5

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the return schema for `verify_attestations(ticket_id)` — specify whether each map value is the full `verify_signature_result` shape or a subset, and add a schema file or inline spec so cc1b and ed26 share a single contract.
findings.finding: `verify_attestations(ticket_id) -> {kind: verdict}` is a new entry-point whose `verdict` value type is never specified. The plan says the existing `verify_signature_result.schema.json` is UNCHANGED and belongs only to single-verdict calls; no schema is defined for the multi-kind map.
findings.location: Verification surface section; AC bullet 7

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/migration path for the no-arg `verify-signature` CLI behavior change (e.g., a `--all` flag for the new listing behavior, keeping no-arg as a `--kind plan-review` alias until callers are migrated, or a documented expand-contract sequence with a deprecation notice in the CLI help text).
findings.finding: The no-arg `verify-signature <ticket>` CLI output format changes from a single plan-review verdict (machine-parseable) to a human-readable all-kinds listing, but the plan provides no rollback path or migration for automation/CI consumers that parse the CLI's stdout. The exit-code semantics also change (0 iff ALL kinds certified vs. 0 iff plan-review certified). T4 requires an explicit back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing, not merely acknowledgement of the behavior change.
findings.location: Verification surface — 'CLI: verify-signature <t> (no --kind) = human-readable listing via verify_attestations (exit 0 iff all certified)'
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce `verify-signature <t> --all` for the new all-kinds listing, keeping the no-arg form as an alias for `--kind plan-review` (its current behavior). This is a purely additive expand step; the contract phase (removing the alias) is deferred alongside 352b and gated on confirmed automation migration.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define an explicit, independently-revertible path for the `retire_attested_pin` removal: either a feature flag that re-enables the blank-event invalidation as a fallback, or a documented mixed-version coexistence assertion (old clones still emit blank events; new reducer safely ignores them without wrongly validating stale attestations) with a test covering that scenario.
findings.finding: The removal of `retire_attested_pin` (a reopen-triggered mutation) is a one-shot behavior change with no feature flag or phased mechanism, leaving no way to revert the behavioral change independently of a full code rollback if the validity-on-read path has a defect.
findings.location: ## What / AC: `retire_attested_pin` removed; no transition mutates any attestation record.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign sole ownership of the `_present.py` `attestations` hex-strip to exactly one child (114f, per parent design); remove the conflicting `_INTERNAL_SUBKEYS` claim from ed26's Added note.
findings.finding: 114f and ed26 both claim to modify `src/rebar/reducer/_present.py` for the attestations hex-strip, creating dual ownership and a merge conflict risk.
findings.location: child 114f-3eea-190e-4972 and child ed26-4ae9-9fd0-411f
findings.suggested_fix: 114f owns and implements the dedicated `attestations` branch in `public_state()` (dict-comprehension replacement). ed26 should explicitly NOT add an `_INTERNAL_SUBKEYS` entry for the attestations path and instead reference 114f's implementation as already covering this requirement.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that the dict-comprehension branch (parent design, 114f) is the sole strip mechanism; explicitly document that _INTERNAL_SUBKEYS is NOT extended for the wildcard attestations path.
findings.finding: 114f uses a dict-comprehension replacement for the `attestations` branch while ed26 proposes extending `_INTERNAL_SUBKEYS` — two mechanistically different approaches to the same strip, potentially producing double-strip or conflicting behaviour.
findings.location: child 114f-3eea-190e-4972 and child ed26-4ae9-9fd0-411f
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the `_INTERNAL_SUBKEYS` claim from ed26's Added note. The parent spec already explains why `_INTERNAL_SUBKEYS` cannot cover a wildcard kind level; 114f's dedicated branch is the correct and sole approach.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC item requiring `_verdict_manifest` (transition.py) to include a `material: <fingerprint>` line in the completion-verifier manifest, and confirm `compute_validity` for `completion-verifier` can read it via `manifest_material()`.
findings.finding: The `compute_validity` validity model for `completion-verifier` requires 'material-fingerprint-unchanged-since signed_at', which depends on the completion signer having recorded the material fingerprint inside the signed manifest. However, the current `_verdict_manifest()` in transition.py (lines 296-305) does NOT include a material fingerprint line — only `completion-verifier: PASS`, `ticket:`, `model:`, `runner:`, and optionally `verified-at-sha:`. No AC item explicitly requires modifying `_verdict_manifest` to add this line. If the implementation of `compute_validity` is attempted without first updating the manifest builder, the material-unchanged check has no data source and cannot be implemented as specified.
findings.location: Section 'Validity model' — 'completion-verifier valid iff: … material-fingerprint-unchanged-since signed_at (the completion signer records the material fingerprint in its manifest)'; AC line for compute_validity; codebase: src/rebar/_commands/transition.py lines 296-305 (_verdict_manifest)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to _verdict_manifest(): `from rebar.llm.plan_review.attest import current_material_fingerprint; fp = current_material_fingerprint(ticket_id); if fp: manifest.append(f'material: {fp}')`. Then add an AC bullet: 'modify _verdict_manifest to record the material fingerprint so compute_validity can enforce material-unchanged-since for completion-verifier'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the unverified assertion that completion manifests carry a material fingerprint: either show where in the existing completion signing path the fingerprint is embedded, or explicitly specify the new `build_completion_manifest()` function (analogous to `build_manifest()` in attest.py) that embeds it, and clarify how `compute_validity` handles legacy completion attestations that predate this addition.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that the completion-verifier's validity check requires 'material-fingerprint-unchanged-since signed_at' and that 'the completion signer records the material fingerprint in its manifest, symmetric with plan-review' — but the codebase shows no evidence that completion manifests currently record a material fingerprint. The plan-review manifest records it via `build_manifest` (attest.py), but the completion signing path (transition.py:498 calls `sign_manifest(ticket_id, verified_manifest)` where `verified_manifest` is assembled by the completion verifier, not by `build_manifest`). This is an unverified capability assertion: the completion-verifier's manifest format and the new requirement to embed a material fingerprint in it is neither demonstrated in existing code nor specified in the plan beyond 'symmetric with plan-review'.
findings.location: ## Validity model — 'completion-verifier valid iff: … material-fingerprint-unchanged-since signed_at (the completion signer records the material fingerprint in its manifest, symmetric with plan-review)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Completion manifest format' subsection specifying the manifest lines the completion signer must write (e.g. `completion-verifier: PASS`, `ticket: <id>`, `material: <fingerprint>`, `verified-at-sha: <sha>`), and note that `compute_validity` for legacy completion attestations (no material line) degrades to not checking material — consistent with how plan-review handles pre-ADR-0002 attestations.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC7 to explicitly cover the `drift_refresh_candidate` and `refresh_attestation` (attest.py) caller migrations, e.g. 'all four identified callers pass an explicit `kind` argument: `claim_gate_check`, `drift_refresh_candidate`, `refresh_attestation` → `plan-review`; `_child_closure_findings` → `completion-verifier`.'
findings.finding: The body explicitly names `drift_refresh_candidate` and `refresh_attestation` (attest.py) as callers to be migrated to pass an explicit `kind`, but AC7 mentions only 'claim gate reads plan-review, close + child-closure read completion-verifier' — leaving the attest.py caller migrations as described-but-uncovered deliverables.
findings.location: 'Verification surface' section (caller migration list) vs. AC7

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit decision rule for blank/unkindable SIGNATURE events and the mirror: specify whether they update `state['signature']` (the current behavior) or are skipped for both routing AND the mirror (the safe new behavior), and add a test for a ticket log containing a legacy blank event between two valid attestations.
findings.finding: The fallback for a blank/unkindable SIGNATURE event (e.g. legacy `retire_attested_pin` blank events already in the log) is underspecified for the `state['signature']` mirror path. The plan says unkindable events are 'ignored for routing' but does not specify whether they still update the mirror.
findings.location: Kind identity + routing section; Compiled-state shape section
findings.suggested_fix: Skip blank/unkindable SIGNATURE events for BOTH `attestations` routing AND the `state['signature']` mirror update — a blank manifest is not a valid attestation for any purpose.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exact manifest format for completion-verifier attestations, including how the material fingerprint is embedded (e.g., 'completion-verifier: PASS\nmaterial: <hex>'), so `compute_validity` can extract and compare it.
findings.finding: The plan states completion-verifier validity requires 'material-fingerprint-unchanged-since `signed_at` (the completion signer records the material fingerprint in its manifest, symmetric with plan-review)' but never defines what the completion-verifier manifest format looks like. The plan-review manifest format (e.g., 'plan-review: PASS') is implicitly inherited from existing code, but the completion-verifier manifest format — and specifically where/how the material fingerprint is embedded — is a new design decision left to the implementer.
findings.location: Validity model section / What section ('completion-verifier') / AC line 5

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Decompose into child tickets: (a) attestations map + reducer + live mirror + old-snapshot fold-in, (b) last_reopened_at + compute_validity validity model, (c) retire_attested_pin removal + reopen-invalidation behaviour, (d) verify_attestations + CLI --kind + MCP wiring + public_state presentation — sequence them so (a) ships and passes CI before (b) begins.
findings.finding: The ticket is epic-scale (9 ACs, >3 files, >3 layers, new interfaces, a migration/fold-in, a deletion of retire_attested_pin, a new validity model, presentation changes, and CLI/MCP surface changes) but has no child tickets. It joins at least four independently testable and independently risky concerns: (1) the attestations data model + LWW reducer, (2) the validity-on-read model + last_reopened_at, (3) the retire_attested_pin removal and reopen-invalidation behaviour change, and (4) the verify_attestations / CLI / MCP API surface. Any one of these could block or need rework without affecting the others.
findings.location: Overall plan / Acceptance Criteria (9 items)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define an explicit thin vertical slice as the first deliverable: land attestations map + live mirror + old-snapshot fold-in ONLY (no validity model, no retire_attested_pin removal, no new API) and gate the remaining slices on that slice passing CI + dogfood smoke-test.
findings.finding: The plan has no thin vertical slice or evidence gate; it delivers all layers simultaneously (reducer → validity → API → CLI → presentation → deletion) as a horizontal big-bang. The two riskiest pieces — the old-snapshot fold-in (correctness of kind-parsing legacy manifests in the wild) and the validity-on-read model replacing the existing mutation-based retire_attested_pin — are not de-risked by any early-evidence checkpoint before the full surface is built.
findings.location: Sequencing (implicit across Migration, Validity model, and retire_attested_pin removal sections)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify (and implement) the fold-in behavior in `process_snapshot` when the legacy `signature` record has an empty or unkindable `manifest[0]`: e.g. skip populating `attestations` (leaving only the mirror) and document that such records remain accessible only via the `signature` mirror until re-signed.
findings.finding: The plan's fold-in migration (old SNAPSHOT `signature` -> `attestations[kind]`) does not specify the behavior when the legacy `signature` record has an empty or unkindable `manifest[0]` — the same case the live path says to 'ignore for routing'. If `process_snapshot` silently skips seeding `attestations` for that record, gates migrated to read `attestations[kind]` will see nothing even though `state["signature"]` still carries the record, causing unexpected gate failures on tickets with legacy blank-manifest attestations.
findings.location: Migration (expand only; contract deferred) + rollback — 'Old-snapshot read fold-in' paragraph; also AC: 'Old-snapshot read fold-in (signature -> attestations)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the migration spec, add: 'If the legacy `signature` manifest is empty or unparseable (unkindable), the fold-in skips populating `attestations` and leaves the record accessible only via the `signature` mirror; `compute_validity` for any kind returns invalid for a missing `attestations[kind]` entry.' This makes the behavior deterministic and prevents gate divergence.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether `last_reopened_at` is updated ONLY in the fork-winning branch (i.e. only when the status transition is actually applied) or also for discarded events; the correct rule is 'only when applied' and should be stated explicitly in the plan.
findings.finding: `process_status` recording of `last_reopened_at` does not specify behavior when the STATUS event is dropped by the fork-resolution tie-break (i.e. 'existing chain wins' — the incoming closed->open event is discarded).
findings.location: Validity model section — 'DATA SOURCE for not reopened since'
findings.suggested_fix: Record `last_reopened_at` only inside the winning branch of `process_status` (after `state['status'] = data.get('status', state['status'])` is confirmed to execute), mirroring how the status update itself is conditional.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split the omnibus test AC into individual checkboxes (one per named scenario) so each can be independently verified and checked off.
findings.finding: The single test AC bullet bundles 8 distinct test scenarios ('coexistence; within-kind replace; compaction round-trip; legacy + new-snapshot cross-read; reopen invalidates completion + plan-review validity without mutation; material-change invalidates completion; concurrent different-kind signs both survive; no HMAC hex in public output') into one checkbox. While each scenario is individually named and observable, they cannot all be verified by a single deterministic command, making the composite AC uncheckable as a unit.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — last bullet ('Tests: coexistence; ...')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the exit code for `verify-signature <t>` when the ticket has zero attestations (e.g., exit 1 with message 'no attestations found').
findings.finding: The CLI behavior 'exit 0 iff all certified' for `verify-signature <t>` (no `--kind`) is ambiguous when a ticket has zero attestations: vacuous truth would produce exit 0 (no attestations, all zero of them are certified), but this is likely incorrect behavior for a gate check.
findings.location: Verification surface section / CLI paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'exit 1 with message "no attestations" when `verify_attestations` returns an empty map.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a body section (or extend 'Presentation') describing the show-command / MCP-handler changes needed to surface `attestations` in output — specifying the rendered format and the 'omit when empty' rule — so AC9 has a traceable described deliverable.
findings.finding: AC9 ('show/MCP render the attestations map, omitted when empty') covers a rendering deliverable that has no corresponding description in the plan body. The 'Presentation' section describes only the public_state() stripping logic; the body never specifies what changes are needed in the show command handler or MCP layer to surface the attestations map in output.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, last bullet ('show/MCP render the attestations map (omitted when empty)') vs. 'Presentation' section
findings.suggested_fix: In 'Presentation', add a paragraph: 'The `show` command and MCP `show`/ticket-state handler read `public_state()["attestations"]`; if present and non-empty, render each kind with its validity verdict (computed on read). If absent or empty, the attestations block is omitted entirely from output.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the 'immutable attestation records + validity-on-read' architectural decision in an ADR, CLAUDE.md section, or named design doc so the invariant ('no lifecycle transition mutates any attestation record') is discoverable by future contributors.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural pattern — immutable attestation records with validity computed on read (replacing mutable state transitions) — but mentions no ADR, CLAUDE.md entry, or design-doc update to capture this decision.
findings.location: ## Validity model (computed on read; records never mutated) / ## What
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief ADR (or CLAUDE.md block) covering: (1) why validity is computed on read rather than stored, (2) the immutability invariant for attestation records, (3) the mirror contract and its planned removal gate (352b). Link it from the `compute_validity` docstring.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the fsck/validate signature check to iterate `state["attestations"]` (all kinds) rather than only the `state["signature"]` mirror, so integrity violations on non-most-recent attestation kinds are detected.
findings.finding: The fsck/validate signature integrity check reads only state.get('signature') (the LWW mirror), so after a completion-verifier signs, the plan-review attestation's HMAC integrity is silently unvalidated. A tampered plan-review record would not be caught by fsck on a closed ticket. The plan claims 'fsck/validate' consumers 'keep working unchanged' via the mirror, but this is mechanically incorrect for multi-kind integrity coverage.
findings.location: Section 'Compiled-state shape + the legacy mirror' — claim that 'fsck/validate' keeps working unchanged; codebase: src/rebar/_engine_support/validate.py line 114 reads state.get('signature')
findings.suggested_fix: In validate.py, replace `record = (state or {}).get('signature')` with a loop over `(state or {}).get('attestations', {}).items()` that calls `verify_record` for each kind's record, falling back to the legacy `state.get('signature')` when attestations is absent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `Depends on: Requires 114f merged` to child 929e's spec.
findings.finding: 929e has no `Depends on` declaration but directly consumes `state["attestations"][kind]` and `state["last_reopened_at"]`, both of which are introduced by 114f.
findings.location: child 929e-1971-6ad5-4fad
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `## Depends on` section to 929e: 'Requires 114f merged (reads `state["attestations"]` and `state["last_reopened_at"]`).'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
